title: Howl's Moving Castle - Chapter One: In which Sophie talks to hats (Fragment 4)
text:
The most interesting thing was the talk from the customers. Nobody can buy a hat without gossiping. Sophie sat in her alcove and stitched and heard that the Mayor never would eat green vegetables, and that Wizard Howl’s castle had moved round to the cliffs again, really that man, whisper, whisper, whisper.... The voices always dropped low when they talked of Wizard Howl, but Sophie gathered that he had caught a girl down the valley last month. “Bluebeard!” said the whispers, and then became voices again to say that Jane Farrier was a perfect disgrace the way she did her hair. That was one who would never attract even Wizard Howl, let alone a respectable man. Then there would be a fleeting, fearful whisper about the Witch of the Waste. Sophie began to feel that Wizard Howl and the Witch of the Waste should get together.

“They seem to be made for one another. Someone ought to arrange a match,” she remarked to the hat she was trimming at that moment.

But by the end of the month the gossip in the shop was suddenly all about Lettie. Cesari’s, it seemed, was packed with gentlemen from morning to night, each one buying quantities of cakes and demanding to be served by Lettie. She had had ten proposals of marriage, ranging in quality from the Mayor’s son to the lad who swept the streets, and she had refused them all, saying she was too young to make up her mind yet.

“I call that sensible of her,” Sophie said to a bonnet she was pleating silk into.

Fanny was pleased with this news. “I knew she’d be all right!” she said happily. It occurred to Sophie that Fanny was glad Lettie was no longer around.

“Lettie’s bad for custom,” she told the bonnet, pleating away at mushroom-colored silk. “She would make even you look glamorous, you dowdy old thing. Other ladies look at Lettie and despair.”

Sophie talked to hats more and more as weeks went by. There was no one else much to talk to. Fanny was out bargaining, or trying to whip up custom, much of the day, and Bessie was busy serving and telling everyone her wedding plans. Sophie got into the habit of putting each hat on its stand as she finished it, where it sat looking almost like a head without a body, and pausing while she told the hat what the body under it ought to be like. She flattered the hats a bit, because you should flatter customers.

“You have mysterious allure,” she told one that was all veiling with hidden twinkles. To a wide, creamy hat with roses under the brim she said, “You are going to have to marry money!” and to a caterpillar-green straw with a curly green feather she said, “You are young as a spring leaf.” She told pink bonnets they had dimpled charm and smart hats trimmed with velvet that they were witty. She told the mushroom-pleated bonnet, “You have a heart of gold and someone in a high position will see it and fall in love with you.” This was because she was sorry for that particular bonnet. It looked so fussy and plain.

meta.author: Diana Wynne Jones
meta.sectionTitle: Chapter One: In which Sophie talks to hats

vocabulary.definition: A small recess or space in a room or garden.
vocabulary.example: Sophie sat in her alcove and stitched.

vocabulary.definition: The quality of being powerfully and mysteriously attractive or fascinating.
vocabulary.example: You have mysterious allure.

vocabulary.definition: To organize a marriage between two people, often strategically.
vocabulary.example: Someone ought to arrange a match.

vocabulary.definition: The activity of negotiating the terms or price of something.
vocabulary.example: Fanny was out bargaining much of the day.

vocabulary.definition: A vivid bright shade of green, like that of a caterpillar.
vocabulary.example: A caterpillar-green straw with a curly green feather.

vocabulary.definition: A pale yellowish colour.
vocabulary.example: A wide, creamy hat with roses under the brim.

vocabulary.definition: Having curls or a curved shape.
vocabulary.example: A caterpillar-green straw with a curly green feather.

vocabulary.definition: Having small indentations or hollows, especially in the skin or surface.
vocabulary.example: Pink bonnets they had dimpled charm.

vocabulary.definition: Loss of reputation or respect as the result of a dishonourable action.
vocabulary.example: Jane Farrier was a perfect disgrace the way she did her hair.

vocabulary.definition: Not stylish or neat in appearance.
vocabulary.example: She would make even you look glamorous, you dowdy old thing.

vocabulary.definition: Feeling afraid or showing fear.
vocabulary.example: There would be a fleeting, fearful whisper about the Witch of the Waste.

vocabulary.definition: Lasting for a very short time.
vocabulary.example: There would be a fleeting, fearful whisper about the Witch of the Waste.

vocabulary.definition: Too concerned about unimportant details; hard to please.
vocabulary.example: It looked so fussy and plain.

vocabulary.definition: Having an attractive or exciting quality that makes someone or something special.
vocabulary.example: She would make even you look glamorous, you dowdy old thing.

vocabulary.definition: Talking about other people's private lives, often spreading rumors.
vocabulary.example: Nobody can buy a hat without gossiping.

vocabulary.definition: Difficult or impossible to understand, explain, or identify.
vocabulary.example: You have mysterious allure.

vocabulary.definition: To fold fabric into pleats or folds.
vocabulary.example: She was pleating silk into a bonnet.

vocabulary.definition: Offers to marry someone.
vocabulary.example: She had had ten proposals of marriage.

vocabulary.definition: An amount or number of something.
vocabulary.example: Each one buying quantities of cakes.

vocabulary.definition: To refuse to accept, consider, or use something.
vocabulary.example: She had refused them all.

vocabulary.definition: Considered to be socially acceptable and good in quality.
vocabulary.example: That was one who would never attract even Wizard Howl, let alone a respectable man.

vocabulary.definition: Presenting or providing food, drinks, or assistance.
vocabulary.example: Bessie was busy serving and telling everyone her wedding plans.

vocabulary.definition: A shining with a flickering or intermittent light.
vocabulary.example: You have mysterious allure, she told one that was all veiling with hidden twinkles.

vocabulary.definition: A type of cloth with a soft, thick surface.
vocabulary.example: Smart hats trimmed with velvet that they were witty.

vocabulary.definition: To encourage or stir up strong feelings or actions.
vocabulary.example: Trying to whip up custom.

vocabulary.definition: To speak very quietly using breath but not the vocal cords.
vocabulary.example: Whisper, whisper, whisper....

vocabulary.definition: Showing or characterised by quick and inventive verbal humour.
vocabulary.example: Smart hats trimmed with velvet that they were witty.
